articles.author: Bridget Brown, Bernard McGhee
articles.description: The Justice Department said Wednesday that it was looking into whether it had improperly withheld documents from the Jeffrey Epstein files.
articles.title: Live updates: DOJ says it’s reviewing whether any Epstein-related records were mistakenly withheld

articles.description: World Economic Forum head Børge Brende says that he is stepping down after he faced pressure over contacts with the late sex offender Jeffrey Epstein. Brende, a former Norwegian foreign minister, said in a statement Thursday he had decided “after careful consideration” to step down as president and CEO of the forum, known for its annual January meeting in the Swiss Alpine resort of Davos. He said in the statement released by the WEF that “now is the right moment for the Forum to continue its important work without distractions.”
articles.title: WEF chief Børge Brende quits over Epstein links

articles.author: Linley Sanders, Stephanie Liechtenstein
articles.description: A new AP-NORC poll finds that as the U.S. and Iran head into their next round of nuclear talks in Geneva, many U.S. adults continue to view Iran’s nuclear program as a threat. At the same time, few Americans have high trust in President Donald Trump’s judgment on the use of military force abroad. The survey was conducted Feb. 19-23, as military tensions built in the Middle East between the United States and Iran. It found that about half of U.S. adults are “extremely” or “very” concerned that Iran’s nuclear program poses a direct threat to the United States, but only about one-quarter of Americans say they have high trust in Trump on relationships with adversaries or the use of military force abroad.
articles.title: Most Americans see Iran as enemy but doubt Trump on military force: new poll

articles.author: Bridget Brown
articles.description: Iran and the United States are waging gunboat diplomacy as talks over Tehran’s nuclear program hang in the balance.
articles.title: Live updates: The US and Iran to hold talks in Geneva as Trump deploys warships and aircraft to the Middle East

articles.author: Sam Metz
articles.description: Iran’s military capabilities were badly degraded by 12 days of Israeli and U.S. strikes last year. But analysts say it could still inflict pain on American forces and allies in response to another U.S. attack. Iran has a large supply of missiles capable of hitting American bases in the Middle East or Israel, both which Iranian officials have named as targets if attacked. Iran may opt for greater retaliation than it did last year, especially if its leaders feel the Islamic Republic is at risk of being overthrown. Iran also has the ability to disrupt trade in the Strait of Hormuz, a key waterway for global oil.
articles.title: Iran has options if America attacks

articles.author: Konstantin Toropin, Ben Finley
articles.description: The Pentagon is building up the largest force of American warships and aircraft in the Middle East in decades. It's happening as President Donald Trump warns of possible military action against Iran if talks over its nuclear program fall apart. Trump likely will have a range of military options. They could include surgical attacks on Iran’s air defenses or strikes focused on Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei. But experts warn that Iran could retaliate in ways it didn't after attacks last year by the U.S. or Israel. They say military actions against Iran could potentially risk American lives and spark a regional war.
articles.title: A look at the US military buildup in the Middle East

articles.author: Dánica Coto
articles.description: Investigations are underway after Cuba said a speedboat carrying 10 people approached the island's north shore and opened fire on Cuban soldiers when confronted. The Cuban Interior Ministry said the troops returned fire, killing four people and wounding six others who were been detained Wednesday after the encounter. Cuba’s government said the people aboard the boat were Cubans living in the U.S. and accused them of trying to infiltrate the Caribbean island to unleash terrorism. U.S. Secretary of State Marco Rubio said it was not a U.S. government operation. The U.S. government said it is launching its own investigation to determine the facts.
articles.title: What to know about the deadly boat shooting in Cuban waters

articles.description: A 27-year-old man has been arrested after New York City police said officers were hurt when they were pelted with snow and ice during a massive snowball fight in Washington Square Park this week. The New York Police Department said in a brief social media post Thursday morning that the man was arrested for “assaulting our officers.” A spokesperson for the department said it did not immediately have additional information, including what the man was charged with. The arrest came days after videos of Monday’s snowball fight went viral online. One clip shows two officers getting bombarded by snowballs by an rowdy crowd.
articles.title: NYPD arrests man after officers were pelted during snowball fight

articles.author: Travis Loller
articles.description: Kilmar Abrego Garcia has arrived at a Tennessee federal courthouse where his attorneys are trying to prove criminal charges against him are vindictive and should be dismissed. The Salvadoran citizen was mistakenly deported last year in violation of a court order. Abrego Garcia was eventually returned to the U.S. only to face human smuggling charges. A federal judge determined some Trump administration officials made statements about Abrego Garcia that are cause for concern. At a Thursday hearing in Nashville, Abrego Garcia will try to prove the animus of those officials is what motivated the charges against him. Abrego Garcia immigrated to the U.S. illegally and has lived and worked in Maryland for years.
articles.title: Kilmar Abrego Garcia seeks to have smuggling charges dismissed

articles.author: James Ellingworth
articles.description: Formula 1’s new era of regulations could upend the series in some unexpected ways when the season begins in Australia next week. Look out for chaotic starts, near-impossible overtaking, and lifting off the gas on straights. Max Verstappen and Lewis Hamilton are the two most successful drivers on the grid and have been critical in preseason testing. They say the cars' complex electrical hybrid systems promote unusual driving styles and make racing more strategic. Four-time champion Verstappen has branded the cars “no fun” and suggested they might be a factor in considering retirement. Seven-time champ Hamilton suggests the rules are too complex for fans to grasp.
articles.title: Verstappen and Hamilton critique F1's new era
